item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

I'm not sure if this is intended to be a secret/easter egg, but as it was pointed up thread, how the SCP object that is blackboxed seems to be very similar to SCP-697. For interest of facilitating the wikiwalk, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-2697-1 is an object of unknown origin and properties, located at an estimated depth of 23 m beneath the confluence of Upper ███ Creek and its largest tributary stream.1 It appears as an amorphous mass, approximately 3 m in diameter, with a filiform extension ~20 cm in diameter that reaches upwards to a depth of 90 cm, just below the soil-bedrock boundary. It is significantly harder than the surrounding bedrock and opaque to all conventional scanning techniques. Unusually high environmental concentrations of argon and cobalt associated with SCP-2697-1 suggest some relation to SCP-███; it does not display similar toxicity or conversion processes, but see Incident Report 2697-436.

To this:

SCP-2697-1 is an object of unknown origin and properties, located at an estimated depth of 23 m beneath the confluence of Upper ███ Creek and its largest tributary stream.2 It appears as an amorphous mass, approximately 3 m in diameter, with a filiform extension ~20 cm in diameter that reaches upwards to a depth of 90 cm, just below the soil-bedrock boundary. It is significantly harder than the surrounding bedrock and opaque to all conventional scanning techniques. Unusually high environmental concentrations of argon and cobalt associated with SCP-2697-1 suggest some relation to SCP-697; it does not display similar toxicity or conversion processes, but see Incident Report 2697-436.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. The two streams converge less than 50 m from the geometric center of SCP-2697.
2. The two streams converge less than 50 m from the geometric center of SCP-2697.

item.title: fussing
item.content:encoded:
but the remaining residues were found to be rich in cobalt- and argon-bearing compounds,

argon-bearing compounds

there's no such thing as argon-bearing compounds.

(Except HArF, but that barely counts because you have to chill the reagent mix (of argon and hydrogen fluoride) to 8 Kelvin, and it's only stable until 17 Kelvin, and then it dissociates into gaseous argon and hydrogen fluoride again.)

I mean, yes, "anomalous", but if you're going to get into stuff with super-complex chemistry in the SCP, then you should mention it explicitly. And this isn't a chemical SCP, it's an environmental one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My thoughts exactly! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would imagine that they'd have several experimental setups sitting in the area waiting for the annual fire, or at least stored nearby and easily transportable once a fire starts, to see what might work. Putting a flammable object in a vacuum/non-flammable-gas enclosure and seeing if it burns during an event would be simple enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sound like that would need some information suppression for containment. It's not like nobody would notice something like that.

That's why they have people in the BLM and Forest Service, but you're right, that wouldn't be enough. Let me revise that.

Wait, the Foundation once made the atmosphere over 150 km^2 of land oxygen free in an attempt to stop the fires? Otherwise, how would they know?

They checked whether the fire would spread into zones rendered anoxic by CO2 flooding or something similar. It did. I imagine it's possible that one of these tests might have happened to coincide with an autoignition event, but as you say, it's unlikely. Maybe I should clarify…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First, the entirety of SCP-2697 invariably burns each year: 100%

Sound like that would need some information suppression for containment. It's not like nobody would notice something like that.

catastrophic autoignition by an individual native organism. Neither .. anoxic conditions inhibit this effect,

Wait, the Foundation once made the atmosphere over 150 km2 of land oxygen free in an attempt to stop the fires? Otherwise, how would they know?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know people consider it legitimate.
It still makes no sense.

It -can- be both.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So from what I can gather, reality is trying to cover up this anomalous stuff, contain it, if you will, by using natural systems like fire and plants and animals.

The universe uses animals to contain anomalous phenomena.

Animals. Like humans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's an expungement style, called yoricspungement. Perfectly legitimate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Only thing that bugs me is

transformative properties closely analogous to those of SCP-[DATA EXPUNGED]plete conversion

I don't buy a Data Expungement that ends in the MIDDLE of a word, makes no sense. But that's a minor thing, since the anomaly by itself is quite interesting. After all is a 'natural anomaly'!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haha, no, it's not weird! Entertaining, but not weird. Glad to have tickled your fancy.

item.title: Re: Author's Notes
item.content:encoded: Excellent. Mission accomplished, as that's exactly what I was aiming to convey. :) (Well, not the part about the Foundation going "good job", exactly; that didn't even occur to me, but it did end up being about what happened. I like your interpretation, heh.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Go ahead, but please please put them under a collapsible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really cool, however I'm confused about whether it is the underground mass, or the watershed causing the fire.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually kind of the reverse, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I assume it is this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Saw it in the forums, liked it, saw it here, still liked it, +1.

Can I give spoilers on the Easter Egg?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The way I saw it, the watershed and it's inhabitants are carriers of some kind of contagion, and the creature in the ground is keeping us safe by spawning these fires and warding us away from the area.
This interpretation of the skip is probably wrong, but what the hell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting Easter egg.

Other than being a little long and not mind-blowing, this is pretty nifty, and the final note does give it a bit of oomph. Also, I was inordinately excited to see the sloppy censor make a cameo. Is that weird?

item.title: Re: Author's Notes
item.content:encoded:
Ahhhh yes I knew if I hung around I would learn the secret.

My favorite part of this is that it feels like a natural response we just don't understand, like reality just patched over a problem using existing materials and processes. The Foundation is like "Hey good job containing this on your own, universe. We'll let you do your thing uninterrupted."

item.title: Re: Author's Notes
item.content:encoded:
Oh snap.

I see what you did there.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Precisely! :D Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh. Fucking cool. +1

item.title: Author's Notes
item.content:encoded:
The thing that startled me the most about moving out West was the wildfires — and not even the wildfires themselves, but the sheer normalcy of them. Oh, sure, a big burn is a disaster… but it's a disaster everyone expects, in a way. When July rolls around, and the steppes and forests are hot and dry, there will be wildfires: in the same way that there's a rainy season in India, or a thunderstorm season in the East, out west there's a fire season.

Aelanna gets a lot of the credit for this one: it grew out of a brainstorming session for an idea of hers. The original idea was pretty different — wildfires are about all they have in common — but I'm still grateful to her for letting me take back my brainstorming and run away with it. Thanks also to Drewbear, Dmatix, and the crew in the Box of R████ for helping me figure out what on Earth this article's hook was going to be, and to Tahunu for in-depth critique.

Finally, there's an Easter egg or two embedded in here. If you want a hint, search the wiki for those chemicals.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[IT'S HAPPENING]1 Evidently upvoted.

Footnotes
1. It's like [DATA EXPUNGED], only with excitement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So these fires are some kind of anomalous response to/defense against something even stranger below the surface? I like the idea.
